title: ML/LLM Engineering & Operations Tools
description: Curated tools for production ML/LLM engineering — observability, eval, drift detection, deployment, cost management.

tools.name: Langfuse
tools.blurb: Traces, evals, prompt management, cost tracking. Strongest open-source LLM observability platform.
tools.our_take: Default starting point for new LLM teams. Self-host on a single VM or use SaaS — either scales surprisingly far.

tools.name: Phoenix (Arize)
tools.blurb: OSS LLM and ML observability with embeddings analysis and drift detection.
tools.our_take: Strong if you have classical ML alongside LLMs. Embeddings-drift visualizations are best-in-class.

tools.name: Helicone
tools.blurb: Drop-in OpenAI-proxy for instant logging — change one base URL, get full request/response capture.
tools.our_take: Lowest setup cost. Less feature-rich than Langfuse but starts paying off in 5 minutes.

tools.name: Honeyhive
tools.blurb: End-to-end LLM observability + evaluation platform.
tools.our_take: Commercial-only; competitive with LangSmith. Good if you need a hosted product without DIY.

tools.name: MLflow
tools.blurb: Experiment tracking, model registry, and deployment — the OSS incumbent.
tools.our_take: Boring choice that works. Self-host the tracking server; integrate with whatever you use for serving.

tools.name: Weights & Biases
tools.blurb: Polished experiment tracking + sweeps + reports + artifact management.
tools.our_take: The premium option. Pricey at team scale but UX gain is real for research-heavy teams.

tools.name: Aim
tools.blurb: Open-source experiment tracker with strong query/UI for run comparison.
tools.our_take: Underrated alternative to MLflow if you find MLflow's UI clunky. Smaller ecosystem.

tools.name: Evidently AI
tools.blurb: Open-source ML monitoring — drift detection, data quality, model performance reports.
tools.our_take: Best OSS ML monitoring tool. Generates HTML reports out of the box; exports to Grafana for dashboards.

tools.name: WhyLabs
tools.blurb: ML monitoring SaaS with whylogs profiling library underpinning it.
tools.our_take: Profile lots of data without sending raw data — appealing for regulated industries.

tools.name: Fiddler
tools.blurb: Enterprise ML observability — drift, fairness, explainability.
tools.our_take: Enterprise-grade with explainability emphasis. Worth evaluating if model risk management is a procurement requirement.

tools.name: Qdrant
tools.blurb: Production-grade vector DB written in Rust. Self-hostable or managed.
tools.our_take: Performance leader for self-hosted. Filtering + payloads work well; ops are simple.

tools.name: Weaviate
tools.blurb: Vector + object DB with hybrid search and modular vectorizer support.
tools.our_take: Strong hybrid search (BM25 + vectors). Heavier than Qdrant but offers more out-of-the-box.

tools.name: pgvector
tools.blurb: Vector similarity for Postgres. The boring, durable choice.
tools.our_take: If you already run Postgres, start here. Performance is good enough for most apps; you defer to a dedicated vector DB only when you outgrow it.

tools.name: Pinecone
tools.blurb: Hosted vector DB pioneer, fully managed.
tools.our_take: Pricey at scale. Use only if the team's bandwidth for ops is zero and the budget allows.

tools.name: LangChain
tools.blurb: The dominant LLM application framework — chains, agents, integrations.
tools.our_take: Inevitable in many stacks. Critique it all you want; the ecosystem and integration coverage make it hard to avoid.

tools.name: LlamaIndex
tools.blurb: Framework specialized for RAG and document QA.
tools.our_take: Better than LangChain for pure RAG use cases. Cleaner abstractions; less framework drama.

tools.name: DSPy
tools.blurb: Programming model for compiling LLM pipelines — optimizes prompts via gradient-free search.
tools.our_take: Different paradigm than LangChain. Worth trying for production prompt tuning that doesn't depend on a single model version.

tools.name: Instructor
tools.blurb: Pydantic-style structured output extraction from LLMs — adds retries, validation, schema-following.
tools.our_take: Drop-in for any structured-output use case. Cleaner than rolling your own JSON-mode wrappers.

tools.name: vLLM
tools.blurb: Throughput-optimized LLM inference engine — PagedAttention, continuous batching.
tools.our_take: Best self-hosted serving for latency-tolerant batch workloads. 2-5x throughput improvement over naive transformers.

tools.name: TGI (Text Generation Inference)
tools.blurb: Hugging Face's inference server — Rust-based, OpenAI-compatible API.
tools.our_take: Solid choice when you're deploying HF-hosted models; tight integration with the hub.

tools.name: Ollama
tools.blurb: Local LLM runtime — manage and run models with one command.
tools.our_take: Best dev/local environment story. Not a production server, but invaluable for local prototyping and integration tests.

tools.name: promptfoo
tools.blurb: Eval framework with side-by-side model comparison, CI integration, and red-team probes.
tools.our_take: Best eval framework UX. Use as the regression suite for any LLM application.

tools.name: DeepEval
tools.blurb: Pytest-style LLM eval framework — relevance, faithfulness, hallucination metrics.
tools.our_take: Clean Pytest integration. Use when your team already lives in Pytest test suites.
